title: Eggs Royale
subtitle: Poached eggs and smoked salmon on English muffins with hollandaise
excerpt: The smoked salmon variation of Eggs Benedict: English muffins toasted and topped with cold-smoked salmon, a softly poached egg, and warm hollandaise made from egg yolks and clarified butter. The salmon replaces the Canadian bacon; everything else is identical. The poaching and hollandaise are the same skills — once you have one, you have both.
sourceNotes: Eggs Royale is a British variation on Eggs Benedict, substituting smoked salmon for Canadian bacon. It is well-established in British brunch culture from the late twentieth century and is now one of the most common brunch dishes in British cafés and restaurants. The technique for poached eggs and hollandaise is shared with the Eggs Benedict tradition documented in American and European cooking from the late nineteenth century.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Hollandaise can be held warm for up to 30 minutes in a bowl set over warm (not hot) water, stirring occasionally.

content.text: The hollandaise is made first and held warm. It is an emulsion of egg yolks and clarified butter, loosened with a little lemon juice and water; the method is identical to making a béarnaise without the tarragon reduction. The yolks are whisked over barely simmering water until they thicken and leave a ribbon, then warm clarified butter is added slowly as if making a mayonnaise. Done correctly, the sauce is thick, glossy, and pale yellow. Done incorrectly (either too hot or too cold), it breaks. The fix for a broken hollandaise is to start with a fresh yolk in a clean bowl and whisk the broken sauce in slowly.

Poached eggs require fresh eggs (the whites hold together), a deep pan of barely simmering acidulated water, and confidence in the timing. Three minutes gives a fully set white with a barely runny yolk. A gentle swirl in the water before you add the egg helps the white wrap around itself. Lift the egg with a slotted spoon and blot the underside briefly on kitchen paper before plating.
Clarify the butter and make the hollandaise

Melt the {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in a small saucepan over low heat without stirring, then carefully pour off the clear yellow liquid into a jug, leaving the milky white solids behind. This is the clarified butter. Put the {{egg-yolks}} egg yolks, a teaspoon of cold water, and half the lemon juice into a heatproof bowl. Set over a pan of barely simmering water (the bowl should not touch the water). Whisk continuously for 3 to 4 minutes until the mixture is thick, pale, and leaves a ribbon when you lift the whisk. Remove from the heat. Whisk in the clarified butter very slowly at first, drop by drop, then in a thin stream, whisking all the time. The sauce should thicken to a consistency that coats the back of a spoon. Season with salt, cayenne, and the remaining lemon juice to taste. Keep warm by setting the bowl over warm water.

Fill a deep saucepan with 8 cm of water and add the {{white-wine-vinegar}} of white wine vinegar. Bring to a gentle simmer — small bubbles rising steadily, not a rolling boil. Crack each egg into a small cup. Stir the water to create a slow swirl, then slide one egg into the centre. Repeat with the second. Cook for 3 minutes for a runny yolk, 3 and a half for a slightly firmer yolk. Lift with a slotted spoon and blot the underside briefly on kitchen paper.

Toast the English muffin halves and place two halves per person on a warm plate. Lay the {{smoked-salmon}} of smoked salmon over each muffin half. Place a poached egg on top of the salmon. Spoon the hollandaise generously over each egg. Serve immediately.

items.cause: Butter added too fast, or sauce overheated
items.fix: Start a new yolk in a clean bowl, warm it over the water bath, then whisk in the broken sauce very slowly — it will usually re-emulsify

items.symptom: Poached egg white trails off into the water
items.cause: Egg not fresh enough; fresh egg whites cling tightly to the yolk
items.fix: Use the freshest eggs possible; older eggs have watery whites that disperse. Adding vinegar to the water also helps set the white faster

items.cause: Poached too long or water at a rolling boil
items.fix: Keep the water at a gentle simmer and time carefully; 3 minutes is usually right for a runny yolk

items.cause: Reduced too far or cooled slightly
items.fix: Whisk in a teaspoon of warm water to loosen; do not reheat directly or the sauce will overcook

content.text: Eggs Royale is Eggs Benedict adapted for the British palate and the British predilection for smoked salmon, which has been a staple of British food culture since the nineteenth century when large quantities of Atlantic salmon from Scotland and Ireland were preserved by cold-smoking and sold in London markets. The dish is now one of the canonical items on any British brunch menu, from café to hotel dining room, and is considered alongside Eggs Benedict and Eggs Florentine as part of a trio of poached-egg-and-hollandaise preparations. It is a technically demanding dish that produces something consistently impressive.
